nativeRange.habitat: Slow to moderately flowing hill streams and floodplain rivers in the Brahmaputra basin; heavily vegetated margins with overhanging riparian vegetation, submerged roots, and leaf litter; clear to tannin-stained water over sand and gravel substrate

decorPreferences: large driftwood and root tangles for cover and territory
robust or artificial plants (real plants will be uprooted)
tightly sealed lid — obligate air-breather and powerful jumper

diet.notes: In the wild, the Barca Snakehead is an obligate piscivore — a fish that relies almost exclusively on live prey — feeding on large fish, frogs, and crustaceans. In captivity, most specimens will accept whole dead fish such as tilapia, trout, or silverside, along with large earthworms and prawns. Many individuals can be transitioned to high-quality carnivore pellets or large sinking sticks with patience. Goldfish and feeder fish are best avoided as dietary staples, as they carry thiaminase (an enzyme that interferes with thiamine absorption) and introduce disease risk. Adults are generally fed two to three times per week; as an ambush predator, the species is well adapted to infrequent large meals.

avoidWith: all other fish (will be eaten or attacked)
any tankmate smaller than two-thirds of its own body length

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Channa barca; verify availability and attribution before use.

summary: The Barca Snakehead (Channa barca) is one of the rarest and most expensive snakeheads in the hobby, with wild-caught specimens regularly fetching $5,000 or more. Endemic to the Brahmaputra basin of northeast India, it reaches 90 cm and demands a species-only setup of at least 1,000 litres. Import and interstate transport are prohibited in the US under the Lacey Act.
careNotes: Channa barca is among the most spectacular and demanding snakeheads available to aquarists. Adults regularly exceed 75 cm and have been recorded to 90 cm, requiring a tank of 1,000 L or more with a minimum 240 cm footprint. The species is an obligate air-breather — a tightly sealed, escape-proof lid with a small air gap is non-negotiable, as it will jump powerfully and can survive surprisingly long out of water. Keep as a single specimen; adults are confirmed piscivores and will consume any tankmate. Decor should consist of large driftwood tangles, robust root structures, and overhanging cover to replicate the shaded, vegetated margins of the Brahmaputra. Unlike many tropical snakeheads, C. barca is cool-tolerant and native to a system that drops to 15–18°C in winter; sustained temperatures above 26°C are stressful. Aim for 20–24°C for long-term maintenance. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12) best approximates the Brahmaputra’s chemistry. Water quality must be pristine despite the fish’s messy piscivorous diet — oversized filtration with strong mechanical filtration is essential. C. barca is rarely bred in captivity; wild-caught importation is extremely limited by legal restrictions in the US and by CITES-adjacent export controls in India. This species is USDA-listed Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act — import and interstate transport in the US is prohibited for all Channa species. Confirm local and state legality before acquiring.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding records are extremely rare. In the wild, Channa barca is reported to be a biparental guarding species: both parents actively defend the egg mass and guard free-swimming fry — an uncommon trait among large snakeheads, most of which show little or no parental care beyond the nest-guarding phase. Eggs are laid in a floating bubble nest or among surface vegetation. Fry are reported to school tightly under parental supervision for several weeks. Replicating seasonal temperature cycling (dropping to 15–18°C in winter, gradually rising in spring) may be necessary to trigger spawning conditioning. No reliable captive breeding protocol has been published as of 2026.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Channa barca
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy, native range (Brahmaputra basin, NE India), water parameters (temperature 15–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm), adult size (to 90 cm), habitat description, diet, and breeding notes including biparental guarding behaviour. Confirmed obligate air-breather status and cool-water tolerance.

additional.name: FishBase — Channa barca
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Channidae, Perciformes), native range (India, Brahmaputra drainage), adult size (max 90 cm TL), temperature tolerance, and diet classification (carnivore/piscivore). Confirmed IUCN status as Vulnerable due to limited range and collection pressure.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Snakehead Species Profile: Channa barca
additional.notes: Trade and husbandry context: confirmed extreme rarity in trade, wild-caught only status, price range ($3,000–$5,000+), species-only keeping requirement, and 1,000 L+ tank guidance. Also noted UK legal status differs from US — not banned in the UK but listed in some EU invasive species frameworks.

additional.name: USDA APHIS — Injurious Wildlife: Snakeheads (Channidae)
additional.notes: Confirmed that all Channa species are listed as Injurious Wildlife under 18 U.S.C. 42 (Lacey Act); import into the US and interstate transport are prohibited. Basis for hobbyistAdvisory legally-restricted flag.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Snakehead Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: General snakehead husbandry context used for feeding, filtration, and tank setup guidance. Notes US federal ban on all Channa species; recommends species-only housing and escape-proof lids. C. barca not covered specifically but general Channa guidance applied.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Barca Snakehead is essentially unrecorded, and virtually all specimens in the hobby are wild-caught. In the wild, the species has been reported to practice biparental guarding — both parents actively defend the egg mass and free-swimming fry, an uncommon trait among large snakeheads. Seasonal temperature cycling down to 15–18°C may be required to induce spawning conditioning, though no reliable captive breeding protocol has been published.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: All Channa species are listed as Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act; import and interstate transport in the United States are prohibited. The Barca Snakehead reaches over 90 cm, is one of the rarest snakeheads in the trade, and wild-caught specimens regularly sell for $5,000 or more. A species-only tank of at least 1,000 litres is required.
